q: Which of the following is considered a periodic motion used to measure time in ancient clocks?
o: A car moving on a road
sol: The repetitive and regular swing of a pendulum is a classic example of periodic motion used in clocks.

q: The basic unit of speed is:
sol: The SI basic unit for distance is meter (m) and for time is second (s), making speed m/s.

q: A simple pendulum takes 32 seconds to complete 20 oscillations. What is its time period?
sol: Time period = Total time / Number of oscillations = 32/20 = 1.6 seconds.

q: Which device is used to measure the distance moved by a vehicle?
sol: An odometer records the total distance traveled by a vehicle.

q: If an object moves along a straight line with a constant speed, its motion is said to be:
sol: Uniform motion occurs when an object covers equal distances in equal intervals of time along a straight path.

q: Which of these was used by ancestors to measure time intervals before modern clocks?
sol: Sand clocks (hourglasses) and sundials were common ancient time-measuring devices.

q: The distance-time graph for the motion of an object moving with a constant speed is a:
sol: A constant speed implies a linear relationship between distance and time, resulting in a straight line.

q: Which of the following is the correct formula for Speed?
sol: Speed is defined as the distance covered per unit of time.

q: The time between one sunrise and the next sunrise was called a:
sol: Historically, the interval between two consecutive sunrises was defined as a day.

q: A microsecond is one ________ of a second.
sol: One microsecond is 1/1,000,000 of a second.

q: The clocks used today for very high precision rely on the vibrations of:
sol: Quartz clocks use the stable vibrations of quartz crystals to keep accurate time.

q: What is the time period of a pendulum that completes 1 oscillation in 2 seconds?
sol: The time taken for one complete oscillation is the time period itself.

q: A car travels 100 km in 2 hours. Its speed is:
sol: Speed = 100/2 = 50 km/h.

q: On a distance-time graph, the x-axis usually represents:
sol: Time is the independent variable and is typically plotted on the x-axis.

q: The motion of a giant wheel is an example of:
sol: It moves in a circle and repeats its motion at regular intervals.

q: If the distance-time graph is a horizontal line parallel to the time axis, the object is:
sol: A horizontal line means distance is not changing as time passes, so the object is stationary.

q: The standard unit of time is:
sol: The second (s) is the SI base unit for time.

q: Which of the following cannot be used for the measurement of time?
o: Shadow of an object in the sun
sol: Blinking is not regular or periodic enough to be a reliable time-measuring tool.

q: 15 m/s is equal to how many km/h?
The symbols of all units are written in:
sol: Units like 'km' or 'm' are always written in singular form (e.g., 50 km, not 50 kms).

q: An object moving with a speed that keeps changing is said to be in:
sol: Non-uniform motion involves varying speeds over time.

q: The time taken from one new moon to the next was used to measure a:
sol: A lunar cycle approximately represents a month.

q: Which of the following is a scalar quantity (only magnitude)?
sol: Distance only describes 'how much ground' an object has covered, regardless of direction.

q: The slope of a distance-time graph represents:
sol: The ratio of change in distance to change in time is the speed.

q: A train is moving at a speed of 120 km/h. How far will it travel in 15 minutes?
sol: 15 mins = 0.25 hours. Distance = 120 * 0.25 = 30 km.

q: The bob of a simple pendulum is usually made of:
sol: A small metallic ball is typically used as the bob.

q: Galileo Galilei discovered that for a given length, a pendulum always takes the same time to:
sol: Galileo's experiment showed the constancy of the pendulum's time period.

q: A cheetah can run at 112 km/h. This is an example of:
sol: Cheetahs are among the fastest land animals.

q: The 'Year' was originally defined based on the motion of:
sol: One revolution of the Earth around the Sun defines a year.

q: If we know the speed of an object and the time it travels, we can find distance using:
sol: Distance = Speed × Time.

q: The metallic ball of the pendulum is called the:
sol: It is technically referred to as the bob.

q: One billionth of a second is a:
sol: 10^-9 seconds is a nanosecond.

q: A faster object covers ________ distance in a given time.
sol: Higher speed results in more distance covered per unit of time.

q: In a distance-time graph, the steeper the line:
sol: A steeper slope indicates a greater change in distance over time, meaning higher speed.

q: Which of the following is NOT a unit of speed?
sol: Seconds per meter is the reciprocal of speed, not speed itself.

q: The time period of a pendulum depends upon its:
sol: The length of the string is the primary factor affecting the period.

q: To find the average speed, we divide total distance by:
sol: Average speed = Total Distance / Total Time.

q: Sundials measure time by the position of:
sol: Sundials use the movement of shadows caused by the Sun.

q: Which clock was famously used in Delhi (Jantar Mantar) in ancient India?
sol: Jantar Mantar houses large-scale sundials.

q: The speed shown by the speedometer in a car is in:
sol: Vehicles in India typically display speed in kilometers per hour.

q: A pendulum completes 10 oscillations in 20 seconds. Its time period is:
sol: 20 / 10 = 2 seconds.

q: The distance-time graph of an object at rest is parallel to the:
sol: X-axis represents time; distance doesn't change, so the line stays flat/horizontal.

q: How many seconds are there in one hour?
sol: 60 minutes × 60 seconds = 3600 seconds.

q: If an object travels 20m in the first second and 20m in the next second, its motion is:
sol: Equal distances in equal time intervals signify uniform motion.

q: Motion of the earth on its axis is an example of:
sol: The Earth rotates regularly every 24 hours.

q: Distance = ________.
sol: Basic formula derived from speed = distance/time.

q: The most common unit of time used in our daily life to plan schedules is:
sol: We usually plan our day in terms of hours.

q: If a car is traveling at 72 km/h, its speed in m/s is:
Which of the following is the smallest time interval?
sol: Nanosecond is 10^-9, which is the smallest among the choices.

q: The position of an object changing with time is called:
sol: Motion is defined as the change in position relative to time.

q: A simple pendulum is an example of ________ motion.
sol: It repeats its path at regular intervals.

q: If a body covers unequal distances in equal intervals of time, it is in:
sol: This is the definition of non-uniform motion.

q: Which axis is the 'vertical' axis in a graph?
sol: The Y-axis is the vertical coordinate.

q: The Odometer of a bike reads 10400 km at start and 10450 km at end. What is the distance traveled?
sol: Difference = 10450 - 10400 = 50 km.

q: How can we increase the time period of a simple pendulum?
sol: Time period is directly proportional to the square root of length.

q: The time taken by an object to move from one extreme position to another and back is called:
sol: This complete back-and-forth motion is one oscillation.

q: Which of the following speed is the highest?
sol: 1 km/min = 1000m/60s = 16.67 m/s (Highest among others).

q: In a distance-time graph, a curved line indicates:
sol: A curve shows that the speed is changing over time.

q: Ancient 'Water Clocks' worked on the principle of:
sol: They measured time by the amount of water that dripped from one container to another.

q: Total distance traveled divided by total time taken gives:
sol: This is the standard definition of average speed.

q: A person walks 4 km in 1 hour and then another 2 km in 0.5 hours. Average speed is:
sol: Total distance = 6km, Total time = 1.5h. Average speed = 6 / 1.5 = 4 km/h.

q: If we represent distance on the Y-axis and time on the X-axis, the graph is a:
sol: It is named by the variables on the axes.

q: Which of these motion is NOT periodic?
sol: Turning a corner is a one-time event, not a regular repetition.

q: The time interval of 'One Second' was originally defined as a fraction of a:
sol: It was defined as 1/86400 of a mean solar day.

q: Speed = Distance / Time. If speed is constant, time is ________ to distance.
sol: At constant speed, more distance requires more time.

q: Digital clocks use ________ to display time.
sol: Most digital watches use LCD or LED displays.

q: What is the speed of an object that covers 1 kilometer in 1 minute?
sol: 1km in 1/60 hour = 60 km/h.

q: The unit of speed 'km/h' is used for:
sol: Kilometers per hour is practical for cars, trains, and planes.

q: One oscillation of a pendulum is from position A to B and back to A. This is:
sol: Correct, completing a full cycle is one oscillation.

q: A bus moves with a speed of 40 km/h for 15 minutes and then with a speed of 60 km/h for the next 15 minutes. Total distance is:
The discovery that a pendulum of a certain length has a constant time period is attributed to:
sol: Galileo Galilei made this fundamental observation.

q: To calculate time when distance and speed are given:
sol: Derived from the speed formula.

q: Stopwatches are used to measure:
sol: Stopwatches are for timing events like races.

q: Which of the following is most likely to show non-uniform motion?
o: A ball rolling in a vacuum
sol: Trains speed up, slow down, and stop, making their motion non-uniform.

q: Which device measures speed at a particular 'instant' of time?
sol: The speedometer shows current speed.

q: The graph of an object with zero speed is:
o: A line through origin at 45 degrees
sol: Zero speed means distance is constant; the line is flat.

q: If a pendulum has a time period of 1.5 seconds, how long will it take for 10 oscillations?
sol: 1.5 * 10 = 15 seconds.

q: 1 km = ________ meters.
sol: Standard metric conversion.

q: The point where the X-axis and Y-axis intersect is called the:
sol: Coordinates (0,0) is the origin.

q: Uniform motion means speed is ________.
sol: Speed does not change in uniform motion.

q: An object covers 30m in 3s. Its speed is:
Which of the following is a repetitive motion?
sol: Heartbeats are periodic/repetitive.

q: Motion of a child on a swing is:
sol: Swinging back and forth is oscillatory/periodic motion.

q: To draw a graph, we need ________ quantities.
sol: A standard 2D graph requires two variables (x and y).

q: The distance-time graph for a vehicle parked on a road side is:
sol: Parked means at rest, so the graph is horizontal.

q: If we divide 36 km/h by 3.6, we get speed in:
sol: 3.6 is the conversion factor for km/h to m/s.

q: A pendulum bob moves from center O to extreme A, then to other extreme B, and back to O. This is:
sol: O -> A -> B -> O is one complete cycle.

q: Which of the following is NOT used for measuring speed?
sol: A compass shows direction, not speed.

q: A 'Century' consists of ________ years.
sol: Standard time measurement.

q: In ancient times, 'Palm' or 'Step' was used to measure:
sol: Body parts were used for length/distance before standard units.

q: If the speed of an object is 2 m/s, how much distance will it cover in 1 minute?
sol: 2 m/s * 60s = 120 meters.

q: Which is more accurate?
sol: Modern electronic watches are significantly more precise.

q: Motion of a falling apple is:
sol: It falls in a straight line.

q: What happens to the speed if distance is doubled and time is halved?
The 'period' of a simple pendulum is constant if the ________ remains same.
sol: Length and small angles ensure a constant period.

q: 1 millisecond = ________ seconds.
sol: Milli means one-thousandth.

q: Which of the following graphs is impossible?
sol: A vertical line means infinite speed (being at multiple distances at the same time).

q: If a body covers 5m every second, its speed is:
sol: Distance per second is the speed.

q: Atomic clocks are based on the periodic vibrations in ________ atoms.
sol: Cesium atoms provide the international standard for the second.

q: A man runs 100m in 10s. His speed is:
